ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for grilled beef rice bowl; closest parent 'beef dish' (FOODON_00002809) used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP covers grilled beef as the primary component; the rice base is not separately coded here. Closest parent used as no composite dish code exists for grilled beef rice bowl.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept identified for grilled beef rice bowl as a composite dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 400 g, comprising roughly 150 g of grilled beef and 200 g of cooked white rice with approximately 50 g of savory sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Beef, grilled / roasted, lean cuts — composite estimate derived from IMR Malaysia MyFCD and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (beef, cooked, grilled) combined with cooked white rice values
nutrient_source.organization: Institute for Medical Research Malaysia (IMR) MyFCD; USDA Agricultural Research Service FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for 'Grilled Beef Don' as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from IMR Malaysia MyFCD beef (grilled/roasted lean) and USDA SR Legacy cooked white rice, combined in approximate proportions (37.5% beef, 50% rice, 12.5% sauce) to yield per-100 g composite values. Energy cross-checked against FAO/INFOODS SEA regional data.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — white rice (boiled) and mixed beef-rice meal analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Grilled Beef Don as a composite dish. GI estimated at 56 based on the dominant carbohydrate source being boiled white rice (GI ~64–72 per University of Sydney GI Database) moderated by the protein and fat content of grilled beef, which is consistent with published mixed meal GI attenuation data. The composite GI of 56 reflects the blended meal effect. Glycemic load per serving = (56 × 86 g available carbohydrate per 400 g serving) / 100 = 48.2.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content approximately 310 mg per 100 g; a full 400 g serving delivers approximately 1240 mg sodium, representing over 50% of the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to half a standard serving and request reduced-sodium sauce to keep sodium intake within daily targets.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (56) driven by white rice; a full serving delivers a high glycemic load of approximately 48, which may cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size, consider substituting brown rice or cauliflower rice to lower the glycemic load, and pair the dish with non-starchy vegetables to blunt the postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat content of approximately 1.8 g per 100 g (7.2 g per full serving) and moderate sodium load are relevant cardiovascular risk factors.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose lean cuts of beef, request sauce on the side to reduce sodium and saturated fat intake, and limit consumption to occasional meals within a heart-healthy dietary pattern.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Grilled Beef Don is a rice bowl dish consisting of tender grilled beef slices served atop a bed of steamed white rice, finished with a savory sauce that is typically soy-based or teriyaki-style. The dish is popular in Singapore and Malaysia, offered at casual dining restaurants and food delivery platforms, and reflects a fusion of Japanese donburi-style presentation with Southeast Asian flavour profiles. The beef is marinated and grilled to achieve a slightly charred exterior with a juicy interior, providing a satisfying contrast of textures. Nutritionally, a standard 400 g serving delivers approximately 672 kcal, 40.8 g protein, 19.2 g fat, and 86 g carbohydrate. The dish has a medium glycemic index of approximately 56 due to the white rice base, moderated by the protein and fat from the beef. Sodium content is notable at approximately 1240 mg per serving, primarily from the sauce, making portion control important for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is a good source of iron, zinc, and vitamin B12 from the beef component. It is suitable for halal diets and is dairy-free, but contains likely soy and gluten allergens from the sauce.
common_user_queries: How many calories in grilled beef rice bowl?
Can diabetics eat grilled beef don?
What is the difference between beef don and gyudon?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Grilled Beef Don as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from IMR Malaysia MyFCD and USDA SR Legacy component data combined in plausible proportions. GI is an analogous estimate based on white rice GI moderated by mixed-meal protein and fat attenuation. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component sources are high-quality approved databases and the estimation methodology is documented and defensible.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the IMR Malaysia MyFCD or USDA SR Legacy entries used for this composite estimate. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the primary component sources used. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the IMR Malaysia MyFCD source. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and trans fat values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy beef grilled data and are approximate. The dish is served at The Rainbow Bistro (60 Boat Quay, Singapore) per field survey data. Cuisine tags from input (Malaysian, Curry Rice, Nasi Lemak) are noted but Curry Rice and Nasi Lemak tags appear to be platform-level category tags rather than descriptors of this specific dish; they are not carried forward as primary classification. The food is classified as Malaysian cuisine reflecting the restaurant context, though the donburi-style presentation reflects Japanese culinary influence. Halal status is assumed based on restaurant context but not independently verified. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (56 × 86) / 100 = 48.2 where 86 g is the estimated available carbohydrate in a 400 g serving (21.5 g per 100 g × 4).
